name: Fatima Al-Hassan
summary: 44-year-old female with longstanding Crohn's Disease with progressive stricturing behavior, requiring small bowel resection. Patient also suffers from iron deficiency anemia and osteoporosis secondary to chronic corticosteroid use.
storyline: Fatima, a 44-year-old with a 10-year history of Crohn's Disease, initially managed well on mesalamine, experiences a severe flare with escalating abdominal pain, diarrhea, and weight loss. Despite a trial of azathioprine and a short course of prednisone, her symptoms worsen, leading to recurrent partial obstructions. An MR enterography reveals a significant terminal ileal stricture. Infliximab is initiated, but persistent symptoms and concern for bowel obstruction necessitate a small bowel resection. Post-operatively, she recovers slowly, continuing infliximab for maintenance. Years of intermittent prednisone use for flares are later found to have caused osteoporosis, diagnosed via DEXA scan, leading to the initiation of bisphosphonate therapy and calcium/vitamin D supplementation. She requires ongoing multidisciplinary management for her complex gastrointestinal, hematological, and bone health issues.

conditions.name: Crohn's Disease, with stricturing behavior
conditions.notes: Longstanding diagnosis, stricturing complications identified in 2024

conditions.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia, unspecified
conditions.notes: Secondary to chronic gastrointestinal blood loss and malabsorption from Crohn's

conditions.name: Osteoporosis, unspecified
conditions.notes: Likely induced by chronic systemic corticosteroid use for Crohn's flares

medications.name: Mesalamine
medications.notes: Initial maintenance therapy; discontinued after escalation to biologics

medications.name: Azathioprine
medications.notes: Immunomodulator for Crohn's; dose adjusted based on tolerance

medications.name: Prednisone
medications.notes: Bridging therapy for flare, tapered over 4 weeks

medications.name: Infliximab
medications.notes: Biologic therapy for moderate-to-severe Crohn's with stricture

medications.name: Calcium Carbonate/Vitamin D3
medications.notes: Supplementation for bone health, especially with osteoporosis diagnosis

medications.name: Alendronate
medications.notes: Bisphosphonate therapy to prevent further bone loss

visits.chief_complaint: Worsening abdominal pain and diarrhea for 2 months
visits.narrative: Patient presents with escalating lower abdominal pain, cramping, and increased frequency of watery stools (4-5 times daily) for the past two months. Reports occasional blood in stool. Known history of Crohn's Disease for 10 years, currently on Mesalamine 1.2g TID. Has lost 5 lbs unintentionally. Denies fever, night sweats, or joint pain. Exam reveals mild right lower quadrant tenderness. Labs ordered by PCP include CBC, CMP, ESR, CRP. Given flare-up, referral to Gastroenterology initiated.

New patient consultation for Crohn's flare. Patient symptoms consistent with moderate-to-severe disease activity. Reviewed PCP's labs: WBC 8.5, Hgb 10.8 (low), Platelets 350, ESR 45 (high), CRP 1.8 (high). Ordered additional labs including Fecal Calprotectin, Iron Panel, and Vitamin D levels. Discussed current Mesalamine regimen, noting it's typically for mild-to-moderate disease. Plan to review all labs and consider escalation of therapy. Continue Mesalamine for now.

Patient returns for lab review. Fecal Calprotectin 850 mcg/g (elevated), Iron Saturation 12% (low), Ferritin 18 ng/mL (low), Vitamin D 25 ng/mL (insufficient). Diagnosis of Iron Deficiency Anemia confirmed. Given persistent symptoms and high inflammatory markers despite Mesalamine, therapy escalated. Started Azathioprine 50mg daily. Also prescribed Prednisone 40mg daily, with a planned 4-week taper, to induce remission. Patient educated on potential side effects and need for regular lab monitoring with Azathioprine. Advised on dietary iron intake and Vitamin D supplementation (OTC).

visits.chief_complaint: PCP check-in, starting new Crohn's medications
visits.narrative: Patient presents for routine check-in after starting Azathioprine and Prednisone. Reports feeling somewhat better, abdominal pain has decreased, and diarrhea frequency is down to 2-3 times daily. Tolerating new medications well, no immediate side effects. Discussed starting Iron Sulfate for anemia given persistently low iron levels despite dietary efforts. Reinforced adherence to medication schedule and importance of follow-up with GI. Ordered baseline liver function tests for Azathioprine monitoring.

visits.chief_complaint: Recurrence of symptoms after prednisone taper
visits.narrative: Patient returns, having completed prednisone taper 5 days ago. Reports rapid recurrence of abdominal pain and cramping, similar to initial flare. Also experienced an episode of postprandial vomiting, raising concern for partial obstruction. On exam, right lower quadrant tenderness more pronounced, with guarding. Given the swift relapse and obstructive symptoms, concern for stricturing disease has increased significantly. Ordered MR Enterography to evaluate for active inflammation and strictures in the small bowel.

visits.chief_complaint: MR Enterography review; terminal ileal stricture identified
visits.narrative: Reviewed MR Enterography results. Imaging demonstrates active inflammation with significant wall thickening and a high-grade stricture in the terminal ileum, approximately 5 cm in length. Proximal bowel dilatation noted, consistent with partial obstruction. Discussed findings with patient, explaining the severity and limited efficacy of current therapies. Options presented: escalation to biologic therapy (Infliximab) or surgical resection. Patient opts to try biologic therapy first, given desire to avoid surgery if possible. Plan for Infliximab initiation.

procedures.name: MR Enterography
procedures.outcome: Terminal ileal stricture with active inflammation and proximal dilatation

visits.narrative: Patient here for the first dose of Infliximab. Pre-infusion labs reviewed, no contraindications. Patient educated on potential infusion reactions and long-term side effects. Infusion tolerated well with no immediate adverse events. Discussed discontinuation of Mesalamine, as Infliximab and Azathioprine represent a more potent combination therapy. Scheduled next infusion in 2 weeks. Continue Azathioprine and Iron Sulfate.

Patient reports some improvement in symptoms since starting Infliximab – fewer episodes of severe pain, and less frequent diarrhea. Still experiencing intermittent mild abdominal discomfort and bloating, especially after meals. Discussed impact of chronic inflammation and corticosteroid use on bone density. Recommended a DEXA scan to assess for osteoporosis, given history of multiple prednisone courses. Continued monitoring of anemia.

visits.chief_complaint: Persistent symptoms despite Infliximab; surgical referral
visits.narrative: Patient has completed 3 loading doses of Infliximab. While there's been some improvement, patient continues to experience frequent episodes of partial obstruction, with associated pain and bloating. Repeat inflammatory markers show partial response (CRP 0.9, ESR 28), but not full remission. Given the persistent high-grade terminal ileal stricture and refractory symptoms despite optimal medical therapy, surgical intervention is now strongly recommended. Referral placed to Dr. Emily White, General Surgery, for evaluation for ileocolic resection.

referrals.reason: Refractory Crohn's stricture, failed biologic therapy, evaluation for surgical resection

visits.narrative: Patient consulted for surgical evaluation of terminal ileal Crohn's stricture. Reviewed medical history, imaging, and extensive medication trials. Risks and benefits of ileocolic resection thoroughly discussed. Patient understands need for surgery. Pre-operative workup initiated, including updated labs, EKG, and chest X-ray. Will hold Azathioprine and Infliximab 2 weeks prior to surgery. Scheduled for surgery in mid-August.

Patient admitted for elective ileocolic resection for Crohn's stricture. Pre-operative labs within acceptable limits. Surgical procedure performed successfully without immediate complications. Resected segment revealed inflamed, strictured terminal ileum. Post-operative course was complicated by a prolonged ileus, managed conservatively with NPO status and IV fluids. Patient slowly advanced diet over several days. Discharged on post-op day 7.

procedures.name: Ileocolic resection
procedures.outcome: Successful removal of strictured terminal ileum; complicated by prolonged ileus

visits.narrative: Follow-up visit 3 weeks post-ileocolic resection. Patient reports gradual improvement in abdominal pain and obstructive symptoms. Incision healing well. Started restarting Infliximab infusions, with first post-op dose administered yesterday. Pathology report confirms Crohn's disease involvement in the resected specimen. Discussed importance of continued Infliximab for maintenance and to prevent recurrence. Continue Azathioprine and Iron Sulfate. Plan for routine follow-up.

Reviewed DEXA scan ordered at previous visit. Results show T-score of -2.8 at the lumbar spine, consistent with osteoporosis. Discussed the implications, particularly given patient's history of chronic corticosteroid use for Crohn's flares. Started Calcium Carbonate/Vitamin D3 1200mg/800 IU daily and Alendronate 70mg weekly. Provided counseling on fall prevention, weight-bearing exercise, and dietary calcium sources. Referred to endocrinology for further management and consideration of other bone health factors.

procedures.name: DEXA Scan (Lumbar Spine, Femoral Neck)
procedures.outcome: T-score -2.8 (osteoporosis) at lumbar spine, -2.0 (osteopenia) at femoral neck

referrals.reason: Management of newly diagnosed osteoporosis secondary to chronic corticosteroid use

visits.narrative: New patient consultation for osteoporosis. Reviewed DEXA scan, medication history, and lifestyle factors. Patient is compliant with Calcium/Vitamin D and Alendronate. Discussed secondary causes of osteoporosis, confirming corticosteroid-induced bone loss as the primary etiology. Ordered baseline PTH, 24-hour urine calcium, and repeat Vitamin D to ensure comprehensive metabolic workup. Will monitor bone density with repeat DEXA in 1-2 years. Continue current medications.

Patient is 4 months post-resection and stable on Infliximab and Azathioprine. Reports feeling significantly better overall. No abdominal pain, no diarrhea. Energy levels improved. Tolerating all medications well. Inflammatory markers are within normal limits (CRP 0.3, ESR 15). CBC shows Hgb 13.0, indicating good control of anemia. Discussed continuing current regimen. Plan for routine Infliximab infusions every 8 weeks and quarterly GI follow-ups.

visits.chief_complaint: Annual physical and chronic disease management
visits.narrative: Patient presents for annual physical. Overall health has significantly improved since Crohn's resection and initiation of Infliximab. Continues to manage osteoporosis with Alendronate and supplementation. Discussed importance of colon cancer screening given long-standing Crohn's. Reviewed medication adherence and screened for any new symptoms. Patient is feeling well and active. Coordinated care with GI and Endocrinology specialists.

Quarterly follow-up for Crohn's Disease. Patient remains in clinical remission. No abdominal pain, diarrhea, or other inflammatory symptoms. Infliximab infusions are ongoing without issues. Labs show continued normalization of inflammatory markers and hemoglobin. Discussed long-term prognosis and importance of ongoing surveillance. Patient is encouraged by the stable disease course. Next follow-up scheduled for 3 months.
